title: Describe, Explain, Plan and Select: Interactive Planning with LLMs Enables Open-World Multi-Task Agents
abstract: In this paper, we study the problem of planning in Minecraft, a popular, democratized yet challenging open-ended environment for developing multi-task embodied agents. We've found two primary challenges of empowering such agents with planning: 1) planning in an open-ended world like Minecraft requires precise and multi-step reasoning due to the long-term nature of the tasks, and 2) as vanilla planners do not consider the achievability of the current agent when ordering parallel sub-goals within a complicated plan, the resulting plan could be inefficient. To this end, we propose ``$\underline{D}$escribe, $\underline{E}$xplain, $\underline{P}$lan and $\underline{S}$elect'' ($\textbf{DEPS}$), an interactive planning approach based on Large Language Models (LLMs). Our approach helps with better error correction from the feedback during the long-haul planning, while also bringing the sense of proximity via goal $\textbf{Selector}$, a learnable module that ranks parallel sub-goals based on the estimated steps of completion and improves the original plan accordingly. Our experiments mark the milestone of the first zero-shot multi-task agent that can robustly accomplish 70+ Minecraft tasks and nearly double the overall performances. Further testing reveals our method's general effectiveness in popularly adopted non-open-ended domains as well (i.e., ALFWorld and tabletop manipulation). The ablation and exploratory studies detail how our design beats the counterparts and provide a promising update on the $\texttt{ObtainDiamond}$ grand challenge with our approach.

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper focuses on building an LLM-based planner for sequential decision-making tasks in open-world environments such as Minecraft. It proposes a method called "Describe, Explain, Plan and Select" (DEPS), which incorporates multiple stages including LLM describe, LLM explain, planning, and goal selection. The system is evaluated on hundreds of tasks in Minecraft. Several baseline methods are also compared against.

STRENGTHS:
- Clear motivation. The proposed method is well motivated from the angles from unique challenges associated with open worlds.
- Excellent presentation. The paper is overall well presented, with coherent flow and easy-to-read figures that explain key concepts in the method.
- Thorough evaluation and comparisons. 5 methods proposed in prior work are discussed and compared against in the experiment section.
- Code is included for reproducibility, although I didn't run it.

WEAKNESSES:
# Novelty of the proposed method is limited to certain extent
As illustrated in Figure 7 in appendix which compares the proposed method against existing methods, the proposed method only introduces three extra modules, namely the "Explainer", "Descriptor", and "Selector". However, their significance or importance are not well justified as explained below.

## On Explainer
At L58, the role of Explainer is to locate the errors in the previous plans. However, according to example prompt provided to the Explainer as listed at L523-L549, it is trivial to summarize the errors because necessary information is already provided and well structured. What the Explainer does is merely compare what items are missing given the recipe, and rephrase it to sentences like "[...] so I need to craft/mine ... first". This makes the Explainer's necessity inconceivable.

## On Descriptor
While the paper claims the Descriptor to be models such as VLMs (Figure 2 and L120), in fact, it is merely rule-based conversion that maps symbolic environment state information to language (L151-152). Table 9 further provides more evidence to this. Since environments considered in this work usually provide rich privileged information that is critical to accomplish corresponding tasks, it is unclear that whether performance improvements come from the introduced module or simply the access to privileged information.

## On Selector
What is the exact setting used in Figure 4 to verify the effectiveness of Selector? At the current version the setup seems to be too easy to be representative (plain biome with 4 similar goals). The non-trivial performance of the random goal selection baseline also suggests this. Further analysis and insights into why the Selector works would be helpful. It's also good to know performance of the learned goal selector. For example, if you represent the remaining horizon as continuous value, what is the L1 distance between the prediction and ground-truth?

# Potential unfair evaluation and comparisons
## On main results
In Table 2 it’s clear that success rates monotonically improve with the number of rounds. L275-276 says that DEPS uses the maximum possible number of rounds. So it could be the case that baselines are not given the same amount of LLM tokens consumed. Clarification on this will increase the transparency of the main experiment.

## On the obtain diamond challenge
Since DEPS is based on a powerful LLM that is highly likely to be pre-trained on Minecraft-related corpus, it is not directly comparable to methods mentioned in section 4.4 that are learned without such built-in knowledge. More appropriate comparisons would be conducted against other concurrent LLM-based Minecraft systems such as GITM [Zhu et al., 2023] and Voyager [Wang et al., 2023]. Including the discussion about them is also encouraged. Additionally, VPT's performance is wrongly reported. It should be around 20% for collecting diamonds and 2.5% for collecting diamond pickaxe.

# Highly variant performance for DEPS on challenging tasks
Performance of the proposed method is highly variant on challenging tasks, specifically, starting from MT7, where the one-sigma range includes negative success rate. Further statistical analysis or experiments to justify that the task success is not from randomness will be appreciated.

# Inappropriate word choice
At L63 the system is described as "zero-shot". Analogy to Brown et al., 2020 is drawn to justify the word choice. However, if we follow the same naming convention in Brown et al., 2020, the proposed system would be better described as "few-shot" since multiple task-related demonstrations are provided in prompts. Similarly, at L129, "one-shot planning methods" should be better called as "open-loop planning methods" given the context that there is no re-planning involved in those methods.

Overall, I appreciate the efforts put in this work. I'm happy to raise the score if concerns/questions above can be addressed.

- Zhu et al., Ghost in the Minecraft: Generally Capable Agents for Open-World Environments via Large Language Models with Text-based Knowledge and Memory, 2023.
- Wang et al., Voyager: An Open-Ended Embodied Agent with Large Language Models, 2023.
- Brown et al., Language models are few-shot learners, 2020.

QUESTIONS:
See Weakness section

LIMITATIONS:
See Weakness section
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
The paper discusses the problem of planning in Minecraft and proposes an interactive planning approach based on Large Language Models (LLMs) called DEPS. The LLM based planner has the ability to re-plan upon failure and can take distance into consideration.

STRENGTHS:
1. To my knowledge, this is one of the first works showing a working embodied agent with LLM planner.
2. The DEPS architecture is quite reasonable and intuitive, and achieves promising results.
3. I think this would lead to an exciting direction to work on. Given the popularity and challenge of Minecraft, I believe LLM solutions might achieve generalizable success in other domains.
4. The evaluation metrics are well-defined, although I hope the authors could link better back to prior works.

[After discussion/rebuttal]
+ This work is conducted relatively early with codex, which has weaker abilities compared to the latest LLMs. There could be even more potential for improvement.
+ This work should be reasonably reproducible.

WEAKNESSES:
1. It is quite confusing that the long standing ObtainDiamond challenge has been re-labeled as MT8 in a lot of result tables
2. The baselines for comparison in the tables do not seem so convincing. I wonder why VPT results are not included in table 8.

[After rebuttal]
1 has been resolved.
2 has mostly been resolved.

Some weakness remains of concern:
+ The authors were not able to reproduce VPT performance to full extent. From my knowledge, the training of the controller actually depends on demonstration collected from VPT.
+ One additional concern is the dependence on prior-works/code-bases might make the model hard to adapt/work on. Cai et al.

QUESTIONS:
Overall, I think this work offers a good contribution, but I hope that the authors would address some questions and concerns for me to change my score:

1. Is it possible to include better baselines, and more details on how the low-level agent is trained/made? From my understanding, this model was built on MineAgent or VPT as low-level agent, but I don't think this was mentioned explicitly.
2. It seems from table 1 that the agent still struggles to get to diamond. What do you think is the main outstanding difficulty?

[After rebuttal]
All questions have been resolved

LIMITATIONS:
See Question 1 for limitations.
Social impacts are addressed adequately.

[After rebuttal]
All concerns have been addressed
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
The paper proposes a new planning algorithm for solving tasks in open-ended environments, and tests its performance against baselines in Minecraft. The proposed model uses large language models (LLMs) to split the large task into subgoals (for which there are controllers that can complete the subgoals). However, this type of planning often fails, as shown in the results. In addition to the planner, the proposed algorithm summarizes errors when they occur and then update the plan, which improves the results. Finally, the method includes a selector module, which is trained to select the quickest goal out of the available ones, which further improves results, especially in long-term tasks.

STRENGTHS:
- Good explanation of the tackled challenge. Figures 1 and 2 are especially descriptive.
- Good coverage of the baselines and ablations to study what changes matter.
- The controller models used in the experiments acted based on image pixels and surrounding voxels, demonstrating that the proposed method can be used with such sub-policies instead of code.
- Proposed method is better than baselines, and appendix results in other environments show the method to be better or same as baselines in the other environments.
- The proposed replanning method is general to work across environments, as evident by how it is implemented and by the experiments in multiple environments.
- Part of the code and the LLM prompts/examples included in the appendix

WEAKNESSES:
- Limited by the set of controllers and their abilities. Authors note that this is a limitation of the current work and could be addressed in future work.
- Some of the parts (e.g., success detector?) seem to rely on direct knowledge from the game. This may limit the application of the method to different environments.
- The "intelligence" of the agent stems from LLMs, and the result seems to rely on the quality of the LLM. This creates a hard dependency for using this method (you _need_ to use a high-end LLM, which might be costly and/or closed-source). Especially in this work, the core models were closed-source OpenAI models, which may come unavailable in future and/or change, rendering reproduction of these results impossible.
- Minor, but more analysis on the failure cases (e.g., when prompts fail, when they work, what seems to be problematic) would have provided more insight on how these types of planning should be done.

QUESTIONS:
1) How were the controller policies trained, exactly? Appendix mentions they are trained with imitation learning p(a | s, g), but where is the data collected? Looking at the shared code in "controller.py", there seems to be plenty of hardcoded actions (e.g., "smelt_w_furnace"). However, I am not sure if these functions are not used. Could you clarify how the controllers were trained, exactly?
2) What "offline trajectories" the selector model was trained on (Line 194)? If the trajectories were generated by an optimal policy that reaches goals as fast as possible, then the training might make sense, but if they are suboptimal, selector may learn a wrong sense of how fast player can reach objects. Seems like this is related to my first question.
3) Line 230: why different baselines/methods use different OpenAI models? I assume different baselines call for different type of generation (e.g., code vs. Text), but this is unclear. Please clarify/specify why same LLM was not used across planners.
4) Table 1: add explanation for what is the "+/-" metric. Interestingly, this is very high for the easy task but not for the hard tasks. Can you explain why?

### Comments (not questions)

- Use "DEPS" (and "DEP") acronym consistently after first defining it (defined again in line 312)
- Not a question/requirement, but seeing results with any openly shared LLM (i.e., weights are available for download and runnign locally) would be interesting to see.
- Another curiosity and not a requirement, but it would be great to see the model in action to see how well they behave. This is not a request, and I realize this is not a very objective measure, but it could give some intuition on how "directed" the agents are, for example (do they wonder around before doing the task, or do they directly go for the task?)
- Appendix line 501: sentence cuts off.
- Appendix Listing  6: first prompt is duplicated
- Figure 2 uses term "HPM", but this is not explained in the figure of caption. Same for VLM (which is defined later). For readibility, explain these acronyms in the caption or the figure.
- Line 224 paragraph: there should be a space between the method name and its acronym.
- Line 289: Citatations at the beginning of the sentence are bit out of place. Did you mean to use "citet" or "citep" commands instead? 
- Line 291: VPT had higher diamond obtaining rates after RL-finetuning (I believe 2.5% was for diamond pickaxe). Double-check what numbers you want to compare against
- Line 292: I'd note here that DreamerV3 used a simplified environment where agent instantly destroyed blocks, effectively making the task easier than what the paper tackles.
- Line 338: A nitpick, but I would recommend rephrasing the final sentence to indicate you are planning to work on this but also indicate that this work lays solid foundation for anyone to build on.

LIMITATIONS:
Authors note the limitations. Especially noteworthy is the mentioned limitation of using closed-source models, and the suggestion to replicate the results with open source models.

Authors did not include broader impact/societal impact section. This is not required for this paper (no immediate impact), however I'd recommend adding one regardless so that authors think of the implications. E.g., these are very capable models, but if they solve tasks in misaligned/harmful ways, they may end up doing harm if ever deployed. We should aim to understand this alignment and/or analyze how model approaches the tasks.


## Rebuttal acknowledgement

I have read authors' rebuttal and new results which did address my concerns, and I updated my score from 8 to 9 (before discussion period closed).
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
The paper presents a novel approach called "Describe, Explain, Plan and Select" (DEPS) for task planning of multi-task embodied agents within open-world environments. The authors identify two main challenges in this area: the need for accurate and multi-step reasoning due to the long-term nature of tasks, and the inefficiency or infeasibility of plans created by vanilla planners that do not consider the ease of achieving sub-tasks when ordering parallel sub-goals.
DEPS addresses these challenges by integrating a description of the plan execution process and providing self-explanation of feedback when encountering failures during the extended planning phases. It also includes a goal selector, which ranks parallel candidate sub-goals based on the estimated steps of completion, refining the initial plan.
The authors conducted experiments in the open-world game Minecraft, marking the first zero-shot multi-task agent that can robustly accomplish 70+ tasks, nearly doubling the overall performances. The method also proved effective in non-open-ended domains such as ALFWorld and tabletop manipulation. The authors concluded with ablation and exploratory studies detailing how DEPS outperforms counterparts and provides a promising update on the ObtainDiamond grand challenge.

STRENGTHS:
- The overall pipeline is useful. The paper introduces a novel planning approach, DEPS, that integrates description of the plan execution process and self-explanation of feedback when encountering failures.
- The fact that DEPS is capable of zero-shot learning, meaning it can generalize to tasks it has not seen during training, is a strength. This makes it a flexible and powerful tool for task planning in open-world environments. However, this is not an entirely new concept, as previously shown in e.g., Code as Policies (Liang et al. 2022), Text2Motion (Lin et al. 2023), ProgPrompt (Singh et al. 2022), Inner Monologue (Huang et al. 2022), Socratic Models (Zeng et al. 2022).
- Beyond that, the proposed use of a goal selector that ranks parallel candidate sub-goals based on the estimated steps of completion is a strength. It allows for more efficient and feasible planning and is verified empirically.
- The performance seems good. The paper also includes detailed ablation and exploratory studies, providing a thorough understanding of why DEPS outperforms other methods. I will include more discussion below.
- Additional details in appendix and source code are provided, which are useful to ensure the reproducibility of the paper and good practice for ML community.

WEAKNESSES:
- Although the overall pipeline sounds promising, there has been several similar work in zero-shot task planning on Minecraft and other embodied environments: e.g., Code as Policies (Liang et al. 2022), Text2Motion (Lin et al. 2023), ProgPrompt (Singh et al. 2022), Inner Monologue (Huang et al. 2022), Socratic Models (Zeng et al. 2022).
- However, the selector is a completely new module and distinguish the work from others, while I feel it could be highlighted more. I list some points in “Questions”.
- The explanation of the selector could be improved. This seems like a well motivated module to have and plays a key role. But, for example, what does this module take as input? More concretely, how are plan $P_t$, agent full state $s_t$, and goal $g_t$ represented? How does it train and what is data collection procedure? More details could be provided or pointed to appendix (not seen).
- The empirical study is great and supports the claims in general. But I have some questions/comments and list them in “Questions”.
- Lack of Discussion on Failure Cases: While the paper mentions that DEPS provides self-explanation of feedback when encountering failures, it doesn't delve into specific instances where DEPS failed and how these failures were addressed.
- Another general issue, dependency on LLMs: DEPS relies heavily on specific LLMs, which may limit its applicability in environments where such models are not feasible due to computational or data constraints, or domain knowledge not available as in Minecraft.

QUESTIONS:
- L122 — Intuitively, the selector seems useful. But how generalizable is this selector to unseen goals/plans? Can it always produce helpful output for selecting plans?
- L241, “This validates that “describe, explain and plan” can estimate the reason for current plan failure and correct the original ﬂawed plans.” — Why this is true? Is there any more concrete example? Why the improvement necessarily mean that “DEP can estimate reason for failure”?
- L245 — The success rate boost from selector sounds promising. I wonder if there is any other measure that can reflect the performance gain, such as average steps?

LIMITATIONS:
Yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
In order to solve the long-term planning problem in the open world, this paper designed an interactive planning framework based on LLMs, and trained the first planning-based agent that successfully implements the ObtainDiamond task in the Minecraft scene.

STRENGTHS:
1. The article is clear and the idea proposed by the author is easy to follow.

2. The work is solid, and the method has been verified to be effective on multiple benchmarks. Ablation experiments comprehensively analyze the necessity of each module.

WEAKNESSES:
1. The failure criteria of the controller that triggers the re-planning mechanism is not clear enough. Can the author explain in detail how to determine whether the failure is caused by the planning errors generated by the planner or the controller executing actions incorrectly?

QUESTIONS:
Please refer to the weakness above.

LIMITATIONS:
Yes.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
DECISION:
Accept (poster)

COMMENT:
The authors present DEPS, an approach towards planning for embodied agents. DEPS uses feedback of the scene and errors in the LLM-planning processes to select subgoals. This is demonstrated on a variety of Minecraft tasks to perform even more complex tasks.

The reviewers generally all agreed the work should be accepted, and I concur. For the cameray ready, the authors should open source code and models as well as list additional information discussed in the rebuttal.
reviews.TITLE: Decision
